skill.name: crawl4ai
skill.description: Crawl websites with Crawl4AI, convert pages to markdown, extract structured data, and automate multi-page web research workflows.
skill.summary: Use Crawl4AI for web crawling, markdown conversion, structured extraction, and batch URL processing.
skill.author: smallnest

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and critical alerts, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, URLs, version numbers, and reference documentation. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill is legitimate crawler tooling, but it has medium risk because it performs outbound web access, executes user-specified crawl scripts, can capture page snapshots, reads or documents credential configuration, and writes crawl outputs to disk.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Crawling and Search Scraping
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally requests external pages through Crawl4AI CLI and SDK workflows. This is core functionality, but it can access arbitrary user-provided URLs and includes a Google search scraping script, so users must review crawl targets, permissions, and rate limits.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and documentation directly show URL construction and crawler calls. This is expected for the skill, but the network surface is real and user-controlled.

medium_findings.title: Local Output Writes Include Crawled Content and Screenshots
medium_findings.description: The scripts save markdown, JSON, extracted data, and screenshots to local files. This can persist sensitive page content if users crawl private or authenticated sites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct file-write evidence in executable scripts. The risk depends on crawled content, but the persistence behavior is confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Session Configuration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents API tokens, home-directory Crawl4AI configuration, cookies, sessions, and proxy credentials. These are legitimate features, but copied examples can lead to accidental secret storage or crawling authenticated content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is mainly documentation and examples, not exfiltration code. It is still a meaningful marketplace warning because users may place secrets in config files.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled JavaScript Execution in Crawled Pages
medium_findings.description: The skill promotes Crawl4AI js_code settings for clicking, scrolling, waiting, login form filling, and dynamic extraction. This is a legitimate crawler feature, but JavaScript snippets run in browser page context and should be treated as untrusted automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation and script examples clearly use js_code. No host command execution is shown, but browser-context script execution is a confirmed capability.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Alerts Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick and external command findings occur in Markdown code fences that show shell commands such as pip install, crwl, and python script usage. These examples do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation blocks, not executable Ruby code. The commands can be run by users, but the static Ruby backtick classification is a false positive.

low_findings.title: Test Runner Uses Fixed Subprocess Invocation
low_findings.description: The test runner uses subprocess.run to execute known local test files through the current Python interpreter. This is a normal test harness pattern and does not include shell=True or user-controlled command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is real, but arguments are fixed test paths assembled from a hardcoded list. This lowers the command injection concern.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Obfuscation Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, high entropy, and bracket-chain alerts are caused by documentation text, version numbers, CSS selectors, JSON examples, and large reference files. No evidence of malicious obfuscation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found ordinary documentation and selector strings at representative flagged lines. The full static volume is large, so this remains a broad false-positive assessment rather than proof for every line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screenshot and Snapshot Capture
dangerous_patterns.description: Crawl configuration can enable screenshots, PDFs, and MHTML snapshots, and the basic crawler saves screenshots locally. These features can preserve sensitive visual page content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The screenshot setting and local save logic are explicit. The risk is content-dependent, but the capture behavior is confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Anti-Detection and Proxy Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents proxy settings, randomized user agents, simulated user behavior, and navigator overrides. These can be legitimate for testing but may also support scraping against site controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documented configuration rather than malicious code. It is still relevant because anti-detection options alter crawler behavior against remote sites.

dangerous_patterns.title: Provider API Token Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: LLM extraction examples use provider API tokens and environment variables. This is normal for LLM extraction, but users should avoid placing real tokens in shared files or examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token references are visible in documentation and examples. I did not find code that sends secrets to an unexpected endpoint.

content.value_statement: Web pages are hard to convert into clean, reusable data. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Crawl4AI crawling, markdown generation, and structured extraction workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Crawl4AI CLI commands for crawling URLs and returning markdown, JSON, or filtered markdown.
Provides Python SDK examples for AsyncWebCrawler, BrowserConfig, and CrawlerRunConfig usage.
Includes scripts for basic crawling, batch URL processing, Google search result extraction, and extraction pipelines.
Supports CSS schema extraction, manual schema extraction, and LLM-based extraction examples.
Documents dynamic page handling with wait conditions, JavaScript execution, sessions, and browser configuration.
Shows how to save crawl outputs as markdown, JSON, screenshots, PDFs, or MHTML when enabled.
limitations: The skill depends on the external Crawl4AI package, Playwright browser setup, and network access.
Crawling results can fail when sites block automation, change markup, or require authentication.
LLM extraction examples require user-provided provider credentials and can add cost.
The included scripts write output files in the current working directory unless modified.

use_cases.title: Convert documentation sites to markdown
use_cases.description: Crawl documentation pages and produce cleaner markdown for review, migration, or knowledge base ingestion.

use_cases.title: Extract repeated page data
use_cases.description: Create CSS schemas for product, article, or listing pages and reuse them for fast structured extraction.

use_cases.title: Prototype web research pipelines
use_cases.description: Combine batch crawling, dynamic waits, filters, and JSON outputs to test repeatable web data workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Crawl one page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the crawl4ai skill to crawl this URL and return clean markdown: https://example.com. Explain the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Extract repeated fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Crawl4AI to design a CSS extraction schema for this product listing page. Extract title, price, image URL, and product link.

prompt_templates.title: Process many URLs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the batch crawling guidance to process these URLs with a concurrency limit of 3 and save a concise summary for each page.

prompt_templates.title: Build a dynamic extraction workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Crawl4AI workflow for a JavaScript-heavy site that waits for loaded content, filters the main article area, and extracts structured data without using LLM extraction when possible.

output_examples.input: Crawl a documentation page and return markdown.
output_examples.output: A concise markdown version of the page with title, headings, main content, and relevant links preserved.

output_examples.input: Extract products from a shop category page.
output: A product list with names, prices, links, and image references.
A reusable extraction schema for similar category pages.
A note about fields that could not be extracted reliably.

output_examples.input: Run a batch crawl over several research URLs.
output_examples.output: A summary of successful and failed crawls, with per-page titles, descriptions, content lengths, and saved markdown files.

best_practices: Review target site terms, robots guidance, and rate limits before crawling.
Prefer CSS or XPath extraction before LLM extraction to reduce cost and credential exposure.
Use isolated output directories when saving screenshots, PDFs, markdown, or JSON results.
anti_patterns: Do not crawl authenticated or private pages unless you have explicit permission.
Do not store API tokens directly in shared YAML files or committed examples.
Do not enable screenshots, PDFs, or MHTML captures when sensitive page content may be present.

faq.question: Does this skill crawl live websites?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use Crawl4AI and Playwright to request external web pages.

faq.question: Can it handle JavaScript-heavy pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents wait conditions, JavaScript snippets, scrolling, sessions, and browser settings for dynamic pages.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: Basic crawling does not require LLM keys. LLM extraction and Q&A examples may require provider API tokens.

faq.question: Where are results saved?
faq.answer: The provided scripts save files such as output.md, screenshot.png, batch_results.json, and extraction JSON files locally.

faq.question: Is Google scraping included?
faq.answer: Yes. The repository includes a Google search scraper example, which may be blocked or restricted by site policies.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing workflows?
faq.answer: Users should review crawl targets, saved outputs, credentials, screenshots, and rate limits before running automation.
